original_abstract:
Spatial transcriptomics (ST) technologies provide a window into the molecular organization of tissues by linking gene expression profiles with their physical context. However, fully realizing this potential requires sophisticated computational tools capable of handling three essential tasks: spatial clustering, multi-sample integration, and cell-type deconvolution. We present GraphST, a graph self-supervised contrastive learning method designed to maximize the information extracted from ST data. By combining graph neural networks with a contrastive learning framework, GraphST learns highly discriminative spot representations. It achieves this by minimizing the embedding distance between spatially adjacent spots while maximizing the distance between non-adjacent ones, effectively capturing the local microenvironment of each cell or spot.

We validated GraphST across diverse tissue types and technology platforms, including 10x Visium, Stereo-seq, and Slide-seqV2. In benchmark tests, GraphST outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving over 10% higher clustering accuracy and successfully delineating intricate, fine-grained structures in brain and embryonic tissues that other tools missed. Furthermore, GraphST is the first method capable of joint horizontal or vertical integration of multiple tissue sections while simultaneously correcting for batch effects without the need for manual batch factor identification. Finally, we demonstrate that GraphST provides superior cell-type deconvolution, allowing for the precise mapping of spatial niches, such as germinal centers in lymph nodes and exhausted T-cell populations in breast cancer tumors. GraphST offers a robust, versatile framework for researchers to bridge the gap between high-dimensional transcriptomics and spatial tissue architecture.
humanized_abstract:
Existing spatial transcriptomics (ST) analysis methods do not fully utilize the power of ST data, because they generally rely on a series of basic operations such as spatial clustering, multi-sample integration and cell-type deconvolution. Here, we propose a graph self-supervised contrastive learning approach, named GraphST, to deeply mine the underlying information from ST data. In GraphST, we employ graph neural networks combined with a contrastive learning strategy to learn the discriminative features of individual ST spots. By enforcing similar features to be close and dissimilar features to be far in the embedding space, the neighboring points are drawn closer to each other while the non-adjacent points are separated apart, so that the local surroundings of each individual cell or spot are effectively preserved.

We validate GraphST on several different tissue types and platforms, including 10x Visium, Stereo-seq and Slide-seqV2. We show GraphST improves upon current state-of-the-art by 10% or more and is able to capture the much finer details in brain and embryonic tissue that other methods are not able to resolve. GraphST is the first method to be able to jointly horizontally or vertically integrate (stack) multiple slides of tissue (data) and perform batch correction without needing to know the batch factor(s) manually. We also demonstrate that GraphST is a better method of cell-type deconviation that is able to pinpoint unique cellular environments such as germinal centers within a lymph node or exhausted T cells within a breast cancer tumor. GraphST fills a crucial gap between understanding the high-dimensional transcriptomics data derived from tissues and directly tying those data back to the underlying, intact tissue architecture.

document.output:

We validate GraphST on several different tissue types and platforms, including 10x Visium, Stereo-seq and Slide-seqV2. We show GraphST improves upon current state-of-the-art by 10% or more and is able to capture the much finer details in brain and embryonic tissue that other methods are not able to resolve. GraphST is the first method to be able to jointly horizontally or vertically integrate (stack) multiple slides of tissue (data) and perform batch correction without needing to know the batch factor(s) manually.
